Football.title: Viktor Gyokeres keeps Sweden’s World Cup dreams alive as he reminds Arsenal what he can do
Football.content:
Viktor Gyokeres keeps Sweden’s World Cup dreams alive as he reminds Arsenal what he can do

Viktor Gyokeres scored a superb hat-trick to fire Sweden into Tuesday’s World Cup qualifying play-off final.

Sweden saw off Ukraine 3-1 in Valencia in a match that saw the striker provide Arsenal with a timely reminder of his threat in front of goal.

5 Gyokeres sealed his hat-trick from the penalty spot against Ukraine Credit: Getty

He took just six minutes to open the scoring with a poacher’s finish from close range.

He added a second shortly after half-time with a trademark low drive to put Graham Potter’s side in pole position.

Hat-trick hero

And he kept his cool to fire home from the penalty spot 17 minutes from time to hand his country a comfortable victory.

His triple took his total for the season to 19 goals for club and country with 11 of his strikes coming in the Premier League.

Gyokeres holds the key to Sweden’s possible involvement at this summer’s World Cup.

The 27-year-old must shoulder the goalscoring burden in the absence of Liverpool’s Alexander Isak.

Sweden face Poland next week for a spot at the World Cup in North America, kicking off in June.

Arsenal boss Mikel Arteta will no doubt be delighted by Gyokeres’ goalscoring exploits, but will hope his £64million signing can return unscathed for the final stretch of the title race.

Arsenal stars William Saliba, Gabriel Magalhaes, Eberechi Eze, Leandro Trossard and Jurrien Timber all pulled out of their respective national team squads with injury.

Former Crystal Palace star Eze will miss around four weeks of action with a calf injury.

5 Gyokeres now boasts 18 goals in 31 caps for Sweden's national side Credit: Getty

5 Sweden now face Poland for a place in the World Cup Credit: Getty

With doubts surrounding a host of key stars going into the final two months of the season, Arteta will hope his side can contend with the demands as Arsenal launch an assault on three competitions.

Arsenal return to action after the international break looking to put their Carabao Cup defeat to Manchester City firmly behind them.

The Gunners were second best against Pep Guardiola’s side and remain without a trophy in almost six years.

Arsenal are in pole position to lift the Premier League title for the first time since 2004, while the FA Cup and Champions League also provide realistic chances of glory.

Arteta says his side will use last Sunday’s defeat at Wembley as fuel to land bigger prizes at the end of the season.

“It’s hard because everyone as a player really wanted to win (the Carabao Cup) and I know the supporters wanted it. The fact that we haven’t managed to deliver it is very, very painful,” Arteta said.

5 The Swede has netted three in his last four league games for Arsenal Credit: Getty

5 Arteta hopes his side can use Sunday's disappointment to their advantage Credit: Shutterstock Editorial

“We've had a remarkable eight months together, and now there's an international break.

"We all go into there with a very hard feeling and pain, and we're going to use that as fuel for the next two months.

“We keep going. It's painful not to give them what they are hoping for and what they are cheering for, but we'll recover from that and go again.”

News.title: Ex-MMA thug, 22, who attacked his ex-girlfriend before Barron Trump made ‘life-saving’ FaceTime call is jailed
News.content:
A FORMER MMA fighter who attacked his ex-girlfriend before Barron Trump made a “life-saving” call to police has been jailed.

Donald Trump’s youngest son called British cops from the US after he saw the victim being assaulted by thug Matvei Rumiantsev on a FaceTime call.





Snaresbrook Crown Court was told Barron called the woman, who jurors heard is friends with the president’s son, in the early hours last January 18.

The 19-year-old made the harrowing 15-second call just days before his father Donald was inaugurated.

He then spoke to police in London after claiming to have seen Rumiantsev allegedly grab her hair and push her to the floor.

Giving evidence, the victim said: “He (Barron Trump) helped save my life.



“That call was like a sign from God at that moment.”

In January, Rumiantsev appeared at court accused of two counts of rape, assault by beating and intentional strangulation, among other offences.

But, a jury found him not guilty of all but two charges – assault occasioning actual bodily harm and perverting the course of justice.

Rumiantsev has now been jailed for four years, two years for assault and two years for perverting the course of justice – to be served consecutively.



The Crown Prosecution Service (CPS) later released a transcript of the call in which police were alerted to the crime by the American.

Barron said: “Oh I’m calling from the US, uh I just got a call from a girl, you know, she’s getting beat up.

“This was happening about eight minutes ago. I just figured out how to, how to call someone. Uh, uh it’s really an emergency.”

At one point, the 999 City of London Police operator told off Trump for refusing to answer questions.

They said: “Can you stop being rude and actually answer my questions.



“If you want to help the person, you’ll answer my questions clearly and precisely, thank you. So how do you know her?”

Full transcript of Barron's call to cops Operator: “City of London Police, how can I help you?” Barron: “Oh I’m calling from the US, uh I just got a call from a girl, you know, she’s getting beat up. The address is (redacted).” Operator: “Yeah.” Barron: “(Redacted). This was happening about eight minutes ago. I just figured out how to, how to call someone. Uh, uh it’s really an emergency.” Operator: “What’s her name?” Barron: “Her name is (redacted).” Operator: “Her date of birth?” Barron: “She’s (inaudible), she’s (inaudible), it’s really an emergency please.” Operator: “Yeah I know, what’s her age?” Barron: “(Redacted).” Operator: “(Redacted) how do you know her?” Barron: “I mean these details don’t matter, she’s getting beat up like I-“ Operator: “Yeah I know but I need to take information from you, so how have you come by this information?” Barron: “Uh I got a call from her with a guy beating her up.” Operator: “Okay how do you know her?” Barron: “I don’t think these details matter she’s getting beat up but okay fine, also I met her on social media, I don’t think that matters.” Operator: “You know I can-“ Barron: “She’s getting beat up.” Operator: “Can you stop being rude and actually answer my questions. If you want to help the person, you’ll answer my questions clearly and precisely, thank you. So how do you know her?” Barron: “I met her on social media.” Operator: “OK.” Operator: “Know the partner’s name or the person that’s beating her up at all?” Barron: “No.” Operator: “And they’re at home, they’re not out into the street?” Barron: “Yes, correct.” Barron: “She’s getting really badly beat up and the call was about eight minutes ago, I don’t know what could have happened by now.” Operator: “OK.” Barron: “So, sorry for being rude.” Operator: “(Inaudible)”. Barron: “(Inaudible).” Barron: “For a reason I don’t know (inaudible).” Operator: “You saw a video on social media, what like a video call?” Barron: “No, No, I (inaudible) a video call.”

Barron then said he met the victim on social media and that she was “getting really badly beat up”.

He added: “So sorry for being rude.”

Barron added more details an email to police, saying he was “racing with adrenaline” and alleged seeing the woman was “getting hit whilst crying”.

It read: “The individual who picked up the phone was a shirtless man with darkish hair…



“…although I didn’t get a good look, this view lasted maybe one second and I was racing with adrenaline.

“The camera was then flipped to the victim getting hit while crying, stating something in Russian.

“The guy had hung up. This whole interaction lasted five to seven seconds.”

Barron continued: “I made two of my friends call the Met Police in the UK, even though they are in the US.

“As per evidence I do not have any, I was told by the victim who I am very close with that this individual was giving her difficulty for a long time.”



Former MMA fighter Rumiantsev admitted during the trial that he was “jealous” of the woman’s relationship with Barron.

And, in court, it was argued by Prosecutor Serena Gates that the 22-year-old called Barron to show “dominance”.

But, he claimed in court: “I was drunk. I was not angry, was not jealous. I was emotional and very exhausted from a long evening.

“I was a bit tired of her hysterical behaviour.”

He recalled his victim’s behaviour “deteriorated sharply” after he showed her face to Mr Trump on the FaceTime call, Rumiantsev recalled.



Rumiantsev told the jury he had hoped it would calm her down.

RECOMMENDED STORIES

He said: “I was demonstrating to her that she would not behave in the same way in front of other people and what she was doing was unreasonable.

“I thought it might bring her back to her senses.”

News.title: Tourist hacked to death with samurai sword in front of girlfriend on holiday island before ‘assassins’ flee on motorbike
News.content:
GRISLY MURDER Tourist hacked to death with samurai sword in front of girlfriend on holiday island before ‘assassins’ flee on motorbike

A BALI tourist has allegedly been hacked to death in the street by two male assassins, while his girlfriend was forced to watch.

Rene Pouw, 49, was visiting the idyllic Indonesian island with his partner when two men on a motorbike approached the couple outside their villa.





The Dutch tourist was staying in Kerobokan, a town in the south of the holiday hotspot island, with his Indonesian girlfriend.

His alleged attackers wielded large knives – local reports suggested one used a samauri sword.

They began a viscous attack, according to the man’s girlfriend, who told police she saw her partner’s brutal killing.



She told cops that Rene tried to defend himself – but he was slashed all over his body.

He was left with wounds on his head, neck, shoulder, lower thighs, hands and arms.

Rene was rushed to hospital by ambulance but passed out on the journey.

He was declared dead when he arrived at the hospital, after losing vast amounts of blood.



His girlfriend claimed she was chased down the street by Rene’s murderer, who was still holding a knife covered in her boyfriend’s blood.

Another witness said he heard screams before spotting the alleged killers.

The suspected assassins are then said to have fled the scene on the motorbike.

One of the attackers is thought to have been wearing a black and green jacket worn by delivery drivers.

Local police chief Ketut Agus Pasek Sudina, said Rene’s partner was forced to hide after the attack.



The official said: “The victim’s girlfriend hid in a dark area in front of villa number four because she had been targeted by the perpetrator wearing the ojol jacket.

“Only after feeling safe and seeing the perpetrators fleeing towards the main road, did she dare to come out.”

Coroner Nola Margaret Gunawan, a forensic medicine specialist, said she had examined the tourist’s body.



She said the popular tourist island has seen a significant increase in violent murders.

The coroner said: “From the first day I started working in Bali until the end of 2024, I performed an autopsy on one murder victim.

“But in the first six months of 2025, I received one murder victim every month, and that was only when I was on duty.

“This is the second murder victim I have received this year, and we’re still in March.”

The investigation into the suspected killing is ongoing as police try to identify a motive.



RECOMMENDED STORIES

None of Rene’s belongings were stolen, cops said.

Dutch media speculated that Rene was “on the run” for drug offences, but he Hague’s foreign affairs ministry did not confirm the rumours.

News.title: Viral tale of ‘stolen dogs trekking 10 miles home after escaping captivity’ turns out to be FAKE after fooling the world
News.content:
A VIRAL tearjerker about a gang of “kidnapped” dogs trekking miles home after escaping the meat trade has been exposed as complete fiction.

The clip – watched millions of times worldwide – had viewers convinced they were watching a real-life animal miracle.





In it, seven dogs pad along a highway in northeast China, appearing to move like a loyal pack.

A corgi leads from the front, repeatedly glancing back, while a golden retriever sticks to the edge as if standing guard.

At the centre, several dogs appear to shield a limping German shepherd.



It looked like something straight out of a Disney film, and the internet lapped it up.

But the emotional backstory that sent it viral – claims the dogs had been stolen for the meat trade before escaping and trekking up to 10 miles home – has now been debunked.

In reality, there was no daring escape and no epic journey.

Chinese state media tracked down the animals’ owners and found all seven dogs belonged to villagers living just a few miles away.



The “injured” German shepherd wasn’t hurt at all – it was in heat, which is why the other dogs were following it so closely.

Locals said the animals are free-roaming and often disappear for a day or two at a time.

They have all since returned home.

The video itself is real – filmed on March 15 by a passing driver in Jilin province – but the story built around it spiralled out of control.

The man who filmed it initially speculated the dogs may have escaped from a transport vehicle, before later admitting he hadn’t seen any such thing.



But by then, it was too late. The clip had already racked up more than 90 million views on Chinese platforms before spreading globally across TikTok, X, and Instagram.

From there, social media users turned it into a full-blown saga.

Some compared it to the 1993 Disney film Homeward Bound, while others created AI-generated movie posters, fake trailers and even images of the dogs being reunited with emotional owners.

Experts say it shows how quickly misinformation can snowball online, even when it seems harmless.

TJ Thomson, associate professor of digital media at RMIT University, told CNN: “Folks are trying to capitalise on existing viral content or trends.”



“Attention is money online and on social media. So, the more attention you get, the more engagement you get.”

It comes as earlier reports – now clearly out of date – claimed the dogs had been “stolen for illegal meat trade” and had marched for miles through freezing temperatures to get home.

RECOMMENDED STORIES

Those reports sparked volunteer searches and fears for the animals’ safety.

But that version of events has now been firmly knocked down.

News.title: Disgraced teacher, 36, ‘used her classroom and car to have sex with student’ as she faces 20 years behind bars
News.content:
SICK MISS Disgraced teacher, 36, ‘used her classroom and car to have sex with student’ as she faces 20 years behind bars

A DISGRACED middle school teacher is facing 20 years in prison after allegedly using her classroom and car to have sex with a student.

Ashley Fisler, 36, was arrested on Thursday after her former student – now an adult – brought forward the horror allegations to police.





The victim told police the alleged sexual assaults took place in 2021 while they were a student at Orchard Valley Middle School in Washington Township, New Jersey.

Fisler was a social studies teacher at the time.

Officials are alleging multiple assaults took place in the classroom and Fisler’s car.

Text messages between the pair confirmed the “unlawful sexual nature of their relationship,” officials said.



It’s understood Fisler taught social studies in the Washington Township School District from 2014 until June 2023.

She is no longer employed as a teacher.

Fisler is charged with first-degree sexual assault of a minor, one count of second-degree endangering the welfare of a child and one count of second-degree official misconduct.

The top charges carry a maximum of 20 years in prison, while both second-degree charges carry a maximum of 10 years.



It comes after a 28-year-old teacher declared her love to a 15-year-old student after a months-long sexual relationship – before being caught by the teen’s aunt.

Jamelah Daboubi, 28, was arrested last April after she was caught carrying out sickening sexual acts with the teen inside a car.

The former Ohio teacher admitted to the twisted relationship with the student as she pleaded guilty in court to gross sexual imposition and unlawful sexual conduct with a minor, WBNS reported.

She bombarded the 15-year-old with thousands of texts declaring her love for him.

Daboubi taught at Horizon Science Academy in Columbus.



After the student and Daboubi were found in the car together, he told police he was in a relationship with her.

The Franklin County Prosecutor’s Office said the pair had been snogging, touching and exchanging thousands of texts.

The disturbing messages were discovered on the 15-year-old’s phone, including declarations of love.

RECOMMENDED STORIES

Daboubi was fired from her teaching job after her arrest.

She could get up to 18 months behind bars when she is sentenced and will have to register as a sex offender.

News.title: Should kids be limited to 1 hour of screen time per day? VOTE NOW
News.content:
POLL OF THE DAY Should kids be limited to 1 hour of screen time per day? VOTE NOW

CHILDREN under five should be limited to only one hour of screen time a day, says new guidance.

The government said that limiting screen time for youngsters will allow them to enjoy their childhood.



The Department for Education guidance says ­that children under five should completely avoid screens at mealtimes and before bed.



It added that tots aged two and under should avoid screens altogether — except for shared activities for bonding and conversations.

If screens are being used, shared screen use is preferable to leaving toddlers on their own, the advice said.

This comes as it was revealed that 28 per cent of children starting reception classes do not know how to use a book.

As kids are left trying to swipe through pages like a tablet, is limiting youngsters’ screen time a good move? Have your say in our exclusive poll.



Love to debate? Join Sun Club for just £1.99 a month for the boldest opinion on the issues shaping Britain.

News.title: Popular baby high chair is pulled from UK shelves over ‘serious risk of injuries’
News.content:
PARENTS have been told to stop using a popular baby chair due to a “serious risk of injuries”.

The chairs are missing required safety information and instructions which could lead to babies falling out of the chair.



The Simpa baby high chair has been urgently recalled over the safety risk and applies to the product code SP05500.



The Office for Product Safety and Standards (OPSS) said: “The product presents a serious risk of injuries because it is missing the required safety information and instructions, which may lead to the baby being incorrectly placed in the chair.

“If a parent were to leave the baby unattended, there is a risk of the baby falling due to the unstable nature of the chair.”

The OPSS has banned the importing of the chairs at the border due to the safety risks.

But many parents will already have the baby chair in their home and they continue to be available for purchase at several major retailers.

The chairs are produced in China by the brand Simpa and are sold as a grey plastic “Height Adjustable Baby High Chair”.

Parents who own one of the chairs can contact the distributor for a refund.

News.title: Cop repeatedly stabbed in NECK while trying to save colleagues in ambush by knife-wielding yob gets award
News.content:
THIS is the grim moment a brave cop is repeatedly stabbed in the neck while trying to save colleagues.

Sergeant Tim Ansell was left with horrifying injuries after hurling himself between the attacker and three officers in Whalley Range, Manchester.





In shocking bodycam footage selfless hero Tim is seen clutching his throat and begging for help after a knife-wielding yob launched a savage ambush.

The terrifying clip from July 2023 shows the aftermath of the attack as the wounded officer struggles to stay on his feet.

Tim can be heard saying: “Put something on my neck here, I’ve been f***ing stabbed here.”



Moments earlier, what began as a routine traffic stop spiralled into chaos.

This was when hooded thug Jacob Brown, 27, suddenly lunged at officers from behind with a knife.

He first targeted Sgt Ansell’s colleague, Sergeant Foster, before the brave cop stepped in.

In a sickening burst of violence, Tim was repeatedly stabbed, suffering open wounds to his neck along with injuries to his arm and body.



Despite his injuries, the officer fought back.

He grappled with the knifeman and was able to grab hold of his upper body to stop him attacking others.

The chaos only ended when PC Marcus Wolstencroft fired a Taser, allowing officers to restrain and handcuff Brown.

Miraculously, no members of the public were hurt.

And in a powerful recognition of his bravery, Sgt Ansell has now been honoured with the King’s Commendation for Bravery.



He received the prestigious award at St James’s Palace this week.

Tim had already picked up a string of accolades alongside his colleagues.

These include the Chief Constable’s Annual Bravery Awards, the John Egerton Bravery Award and the North West Bravery Award.



Police said: “The actions of all three officers ultimately saved each other and meant no members of the public suffered any injuries.”

Brown had no connection to the original crash.

He can be seen on bodycam footage approaching the officers despite not being involved in the crash incident.

Dressed all in black with a hood tightly fastened, he is seen walking down the street near his home before jumping into the road to attack the officers from behind.



PS Foster was struck to the head several times as Brown aimed a knife at his neck.

Brown was armed with a blue-handled craft knife and later admitted attempted murder and three counts of wounding with intent.

He was sentenced in April 2024 to an indefinite hospital order.

Prior to sentencing and while in custody, Brown stabbed a staff member during an induction at HMP Liverpool.

RECOMMENDED STORIES

The following month after that incident Brown attacked a prison guard and stabbed him in the neck with a sharp object.



But it is Sgt Ansell’s courage in the face of a brutal, unprovoked attack that has now been rightly recognised after he risked everything to protect his fellow officers.

News.title: Boy, 16, pleads not guilty to murder of schoolgirl, 9, stabbed in chest
News.content:
IN DOCK Boy, 16, pleads not guilty to murder of schoolgirl, 9, stabbed in chest

A 16-YEAR-old boy has pleaded not guilty to murdering a “fun-loving” nine-year-old girl who was found stabbed to death.

Little Aria died from a single knife wound in a tragedy that rocked the seaside town of Weston-super-Mare, Somerset, last December.





Cops were scrambled to a property at around 6.09pm on Monday December 15, arresting a teen on suspicion of murder seven minutes later.

A 16-year-old boy, then 15, who cannot be named for legal reasons, was remanded into youth detention accommodation charged with murder.

Appearing at Bristol Crown Court today, he pleaded not guilty to murder.

He replied “not guilty” after the single charge against him was read out.



Prosecuting, Ray Tully KC told the court: “The matter is set for trial on June 15, with a time estimate of two weeks.”

Judge Martin Picton said reporting restrictions preventing the identity of the defendant from being published would continue, due to his age.

Aria had tragically celebrated her ninth birthday with a KPop Demon Hunters party just days before her death.

Her devasted relatives hailed her as “brave” and “kind-hearted”, adding “our hearts are broken in a way we never imagined possible”.



Her mum Victoria Hull, a former soldier and hairstylist, was said to be devastated.

Family on her mum’s side said: “For those who didn’t have the privilege of knowing Aria, she was the most beautiful little soul…

“She loved to sing and dance, and she took such pride in dressing up, always wanting to look just like her mummy.

“The loss of Aria has devastated us beyond words. Our hearts are broken in a way we never imagined possible.”

Hundreds of devastated locals attended a candle-lit vigil on the beach in the days after her tragic death.



Her dad, Tom Thorpe, called his daughter a “special little angel” in an emotional tribute.

He said: “It’s hard to put into words how devastating it is, our little Aria, that you’re up there with the angels looking over us.

“You were such a brave, kind-hearted and a beautiful innocent soul.

“You always put a smile on people’s faces with your wacky ways and just outright madness.

“You always found it so easy to show your affection to others and ensure others around you were happy and never bored!



RECOMMENDED STORIES

“A life gone far too early but I hope a life lived well. We all love you dearly. Goodnight darling.”

The boy, who was remanded into custody, will stand trial at Bristol Crown Court on June 15.

News.title: Cops accused of being 'fixed' on Lucy Letby & may have broken law during probe
News.content:
Cops accused of being 'fixed' on Lucy Letby & may have broken law during probe

Questions have been raised about the investigation into Lucy Letby, who was convicted of seven counts of murder and eight of attempted murder, which saw her handed 15 whole life orders

View 3 Images The investigation into Lucy Letby was "flawed", an MP has claimed (Image: Chester Standard / SWNS.com)

The investigation into Lucy Letby was "flawed" and could even have seen rules broken, an MP has claimed. The conviction of the former neonatal nurse left the UK shocked, as she was found in court to be guilty of murdering seven babies, and attempting to murder seven others.

‌



The nurse is currently serving 15 whole life orders behind bars, which means there is no possibility she will ever be released, but the investigation into Letby has come under increasing scrutiny. Reports and documentaries have examined the case, and medical experts have alleged that she was not the prolific murderer that abused her position of trust as she was portrayed by the prosecution.

‌



Now, Tory MP Sir David Davis has gone even further, blasting Cheshire Police and alleging that they "cherry-picked" information that painted her in a bad light, and that they were 'fixed' on proving her guilt, rather than examining every possible suspect, or reason behind the tragic deaths on the neonatal unit between 2015 and 2016. The MP also compared Letby to another woman who was convicted of murdering her two young sons all the way back in 1998, before being cleared during an appeal four years later - the case investigated by the same police force.

‌



Disturbing link between Lucy Letby and original Angel of Death nurse Beverley Allitt READ MORE :

"Justice demands that the police look at everyone. It does not permit them to fix on one individual and build a case around them," Sir Davis said, as he called for Letby to face a retrial. He alleged that Cheshire police have "broken the rules, disregarded all relevant safeguards, and committed clear and serious departures from statutory guidance".

The MP pointed out that two former police officers with many years experience between them at first believed Letby to be guilty, but after looking over the case think "she was the victim of a serious miscarriage of justice after examining the hard facts". The officers he cited are also reportedly experts in healthcare crimes: an ex-Assistant Chief Constable, Dr Steve Watts, who penned the National Police Guidelines on how to investigate any deaths that occur in healthcare settings and Detective Superintendent Stuart Clifton - now retired - who caught Beverley Allitt in 1991 after she killed three young children at a hospital, a crime that caused her to be given the nickname of the 'Angel of Death'.

View 3 Images David Davis has questioned Lucy Letby's convictions (Image: Courtesy of Netflix)

‌



Speaking in Parliament, the MP told his cohort that the 'failing' of the neonatal unit where Letby worked were not given enough focus during the police investigation. He pointed out that the unit at the Countess of Chester Hospital was downgraded to "level one" in 2016, which meant that they were not allowed to admit seriously unwell babies and accused the hospital of "at best inadequate, and at worst, appalling" clinical management procedures.

He cited other reviews of the hospital, including one from the Royal College of Paediatricians that said there had been "numerous failures of medical care" at the facility. Despite all of this, the MP claimed, Letby became the singular "focus of suspicion" for the police, though he alleged the doctors who worked with her and testified against her during her trial "could very well have contributed to the spikes in deaths attributed to Letby."

Further, quoting Dr Watts, Sir Davis said: "Where was the decision not to treat the doctors as suspects, or the other nurses, or the cleaner?" He also slammed the use of one particular expert by the investigators Dr Dewi Evans, who he alleged adapts his testimony to whatever the prosecution case might be. After 10 minutes of reviewing, the Sun reports, Dr Evans claimed he knew that something was fishy about Letby case files, something the MP says should have been seen as a red flag.

‌



He also criticised the investigators for not using more than one expert medical witness, and said that Cheshire Police did not look at every possible line of enquiry. One particularly crucial piece of evidence in the trial - a table that showed the deaths that had taken place when Letby was on shift at the hospital - was held up as an example of how the MP believes skewed evidence was presented to the jury.

"Cheshire Police, who compiled the table, chose only to highlight the shifts during which Letby was present, while disregarding similar events when she was not. The seven deaths ultimately charged as murders were, in effect, selected because she just so happened to be on duty. As Stuart Clifton bluntly puts it, 'Police cherry-picked rota cases to match Letby's shifts'."

View 3 Images Letby's convictions continue to be the topic of fierce debate

‌



The MP continued: "They failed to pursue alternative lines of inquiry. They failed to refer the case to the appropriate specialist bodies, including the Serious Crime Division.

"They failed to follow established guidance in their appointment of expert witnesses. They failed to engage with complex statistical evidence, thus failing to correctly inform the jury."

Sir David said he will be writing to the Director of Public Prosecutions about all the issues he perceives in the case - many of which echo what Letby's defence team said during her trial.

Article continues below

Ben Myers KC acting for Letby told the court that between 2015 and 2016 the hospital had repeatedly "failed" and "blame for absolutely everything" that went wrong was "heaped" upon his client. In his closing speech he told the court that his client was innocent and that the reality was she worked in "understaffed and overstretched" unit.

Replying on behalf of the government, policing minister Sarah Jones said Letby had been convicted following "a proper process” and that Cheshire police had been awarded "some of the highest ratings in the country” by the police inspectorate.

She continued: "I just want to end by reminding the house this country uses due process, there has been due process, that has been followed in the convictions of Lucy Letby with a trial by jury, and upheld on appeal. And I remain confident of that and also of the effectiveness of the Cheshire constabulary.”

News.title: 'I worked for Donald Trump and warned him of upcoming disaster - his response was bizarre'
News.content:
'I worked for Donald Trump and warned him of upcoming disaster - his response was bizarre'

A former Trump administration official has commented on the current situation in the Middle East by reflecting on what Trump was like during a different crisis in his first term

View 3 Images Someone who worked with Donald Trump has spoken about what he was like in his first term (Image: Getty)

As the conflict continues in the Middle East, there are growing questions over what Donald Trump’s plan is for the war, as the controversial leader simultaneously talks about peace talks whilst also reportedly considering sending up to 10,000 extra troops to the region.

‌



With so much contradicting information flying around, it is hard for many to get a read on what exactly is going on with the direction of America’s war with Iran.

‌



Some have attempted to try and understand Trump’s second term by looking back at his first term for clues as to what he might do next.

‌



One individual, who worked with Trump during his first term, has talked about one example of Trump’s confusing behaviour during crisis situations.

Miles Taylor has written about how Trump reacted before a Category 5 hurricane, the strongest type of the meteorological phenomenon, was about to strike the the USA. Mr. Taylor said during a meeting about the matter that Trump did something “jarring.”

View 3 Images Donald Trump and Marco Rubio (Image: Getty)

‌



Writing in The i, he said Trump went off on a series of tangents before eventually being drawn back to the topic at hand.

Mr. Taylor said: “He started talking about helicopters. Specifically, he wanted to share with us his frustration that helicopters are always breaking down because, in his words, ‘there are too many parts!’ Mid-briefing.

“We were asking him to issue a warning to Americans to evacuate the affected area, and he went off on a tangent about helicopters. And then another about the election. We finally got him back on track, but the clock was ticking.”

‌



Mr. Taylor said that while at the time he “filed it away,” with hindsight, “it was an eye-opening preview.”

Mr. Taylor’s own reflections came at a crucial time as uncertainty over what Trump’s plan for Iran reaches new levels.

View 3 Images America is continuing to build-up its military assets in the Middle East (Image: Getty)

Article continues below

Although Trump has talked up a potential cessation of hostilities between the USA and Iran, going so far as to extend a pause on striking Iranian energy plants to April 6, recent reports suggest he is considering a massive troop deployment to the region. For context, Trump has already sent the 82nd Airborne Division and around 5,000 Marines.

On whether a decision had been taken to send more soldiers to the Middle East, spokeswoman for the White House Anna Kelly said no decision had been taken as of yet.

She said: “All announcements regarding troop deployments will come from the Department of War. As we have said, President Trump always has all military options at his disposal.”

News.title: Vauxhall, Fiat, BMW, Hyundai, Ford warning as buyers 'make mistake'
News.content:
Vauxhall, Fiat, BMW, Hyundai, Ford warning as buyers 'make mistake'

Buyers have been urged to be aware

View 3 Images BMW was on the list (Image: Sue Thatcher via Getty Images)

Drivers have been issued a new warning. One in five used cars has a damage history, new research shows, as experts warn that "buyers should not rely on appearance alone" when purchasing a new motor. The UK’s most damaged used cars of 2025 have been revealed, with Vauxhall topping the national rankings, according to new figures from vehicle history platform carVertical.

‌



A quarter of Vauxhalls (24.7 per cent) checked in the UK had at least one recorded damage event, making it the most frequently damaged brand among those analysed. They were followed by Fiat (23.6 per cent), SEAT (23.5 per cent), Hyundai (23 per cent), and Ford (22.6 per cent), suggesting that many of the cars British drivers buy for affordability and everyday practicality are also the ones most likely to show signs of past damage.

‌



Across all vehicles checked by carVertical in the UK, one in five (20.3 per cent) had damage history, while the average value of a single recorded damage event was £4,800. Bentley recorded the highest average damage value at £16,000, despite being among the least frequently damaged brands in the UK, while Tesla (£5,700) and BMW (£5,200) also stood out for high average damage costs.

‌



Among individual models, smaller cars are the ones most likely to show damage history. The SEAT Ibiza was the most frequently damaged model in the UK, with 33.6 per cent of those checked showing damage history, followed by the Audi A1 (30.6 per cent) and Fiat 500 (30.1 per cent).

Matas Buzelis, motoring expert at carVertical, said a history of serious damage did not automatically make a car unsafe, but it should change the level of scrutiny.

View 3 Images Vauxhall topped the list (Image: Sue Thatcher via Getty Images)

‌



He added: “Damage is one of the most common issues buyers can encounter in the used car market and in many cases it affects exactly the sort of models people shop for every day - affordable, practical cars from familiar brands. A damaged car is not automatically a bad car, but buyers need to be aware of how serious the damage was and how well it was repaired. Two vehicles can look almost identical in an advert, while one has a much more complicated history underneath.

“That is why buyers should not rely on appearance alone. A car can be freshly cleaned, well photographed and priced attractively, but still have a record of accidents, poor-quality repairs, or even a previous insurance write-off classification. Without checking its history, it is very easy to miss that.

“One of the biggest mistakes buyers make is only checking the final car they are about to buy. It is much safer to compare several options side by side and rule out the ones with the worst histories early in the process. Leave no car unchecked.

‌



“When buyers compare more than one vehicle, it becomes easier to spot what looks normal for the age, mileage, and price and what does not. That is where vehicle history checks can help, by giving consumers better context before they commit to a purchase.”

Nouran Moustafa, practice principal and IFA at Roxton Wealth, said buyers needed to run a full vehicle history check before buying a car.

She added: "This is exactly why buyers should never confuse 'looks fine' with 'is fine'. Used cars can be one of the easiest places to make an expensive mistake, because cosmetic repairs can hide bigger structural or mechanical problems underneath.

‌



"The fact that so many everyday, practical brands are showing recorded damage does not mean they are bad cars, but it does mean buyers need to slow down and do proper checks instead of getting carried away by price alone. My advice is simple: always run a full vehicle history check, inspect the panel gaps, paint consistency, tyres, lights and shut lines, and never skip an independent inspection if something feels off.

"The biggest warning sign is usually when the price looks too good for the age, mileage and condition. A cheap used car can become a very expensive car if the damage underneath was never properly repaired."

View 3 Images Second-hand Ford buyers also need to take care (Image: Sue Thatcher via Getty Images)

‌



Rohit Parmar-Mistry, founder at Burton-on-Trent-based Pattrn Data, said a dealer getting defensive about paperwork is a red flag.

He added: "‘One in five’ isn’t a shocking stat, it’s a reminder that the used car market runs on incomplete information. Damage gets repaired, records get missed and sellers optimise for speed, not truth.

"The practical move is to treat every used car like a mini due diligence exercise: get a full history check, look for mismatched paint or panel gaps, watch for uneven tyre wear, and budget for an independent inspection. If a dealer gets defensive about paperwork, walk away. The cheapest car is often the most expensive after month two. The bigger issue is data quality.

Article continues below

"We audit automated decision systems all the time and the pattern is the same: people treat a report as ‘the truth’ when it’s really ‘the truth plus the gaps’. A clean report does not mean no damage, it means no recorded damage. Buyers should ask what would have to be true for this check to miss something, and who benefits from that blind spot."

News.title: Police swarm home in Birmingham as man 'shot twice on doorstep'
News.content:
Police have launched an investigation after a man was shot on a doorstep in a busy residential area.

The victim, aged in his 30s, was attacked at the door of a property in Montgomery Street, Sparkbrook, at around 7.20pm yesterday. He was rushed to hospital with chest and shoulder injuries. The gunman arrived and left the scene in a black vehicle, West Midlands Police said.

The force have examined CCTV footage, spoken to witnesses and increased patrols in the area since the attack.

A scene remained in place today where a cafe within the cordon, C. Kay's Diner, appeared to have bullet holes in the glass of its front door, Birmingham Live reports.

West Midlands Ambulance Service said in an earlier statement: "We were called to reports of a shooting at an address on Montgomery Street in Sparkbrook at 7.39pm and sent an ambulance to the scene. On arrival, crews found a man who they treated for serious injuries before conveying him to hospital."

A full statement from West Midlands Police reads: "We're investigating after shots were fired on a street in Birmingham last night. It's believed a gun was fired at the door of a property on Montgomery Street, at around 7.20pm.

"One man in his 30s suffered injuries to his chest and shoulder, which were not life-changing or threatening. It's believed the person who fired the gun arrived and left in a black vehicle.

"Our Major Crime Unit is investigating, with CCTV being examined and witnesses being spoken to. Extra officers are in the area today, and we’d encourage anyone with information to get in touch via 101 or Live Chat. Quote log 4962 of March 27. Anyone with information to get in touch via 101 or Live Chat."

News.title: Burgers recalled from supermarkets as customers warned 'do not eat'
News.content:
Burgers recalled from supermarkets as customers warned 'do not eat'

A batch of burgers has been taken off the shelves following an urgent recall from the supplier

View 2 Images Premium meat brand Heck issued a recall for a certain batch of burgers after a packaging mix-up that poses a risk to customers with milk allergies (Image: LordHenriVoton via Getty Images)

A well-known premium meat brand has announced a product recall affecting a specific batch of one of its items. Heck, renowned for its pork and chicken sausages and burgers, has confirmed that a batch of one of its burgers has been pulled from shelves following a packaging blunder.

‌



The brand, which is available at major supermarkets including Tesco and Waitrose, explained that the packaging error resulted in certain batches of the Heck Minted Lamb Burgers being swapped with an entirely different product. This potentially puts customers with allergies and intolerances to milk at risk.

‌



The affected batches were incorrectly packaged with Heck Steak and Butter Burger, while displaying the Minted Lamb sleeve.

‌



This mix-up could pose a serious risk to customers with milk or milk constituent allergies or intolerances, as the Heck Steak and Butter Burger contains milk, according to the Express.

View 2 Images A certain batch of the Heck Minted Lamb Burgers 2 Pack 320g were incorrectly packaged with another item (Image: Heck)

Shoppers who have purchased the affected batch are being urged not to consume them, particularly those with an allergy or intolerance to milk or its constituents.

‌



No other Heck products are affected by this recall.

The specific product affected by the recall is listed below, along with its use-by date:

Heck Minted Lamb Burgers 2 Pack 320g

Use by date: 03/04/26

Article continues below

Anyone who has bought the affected batch of Heck burgers is encouraged to return the item to the place of purchase for a full refund.

For further information regarding this recall, customers are advised to contact Heck directly on 01845 567 709 or via email at sausages@heckfood.co.uk.

Heck has been approached for comment.

News.title: Woman left paralysed by gang rape issued three-word message before being euthanised
News.content:
Woman left paralysed by gang rape issued three-word message before being euthanised

A 25-year-old woman from Barcelona will be euthanised after Spain's Constitutional Court approved her request following a brutal gang rape in 2022 that led to her becoming paralysed

View 2 Images Ramos tried to end her life by jumping from a fifth-story window (Image: Antena 3)

A young woman who jumped from a fifth-floor window following a devastating sexual assault issued a three word message before ending her life by euthanasia.

‌



Speaking in an interview just days before her death, Noelia Castillo Ramos, 25, said she simply wanted to "leave in peace" after enduring years of pain and suffering. The young Barcelona woman received approval from both the European Court of Human Rights and Spain's Constitutional Court. She died yesterday evening.

‌



Ramos suffered a severe spinal cord injury after leaping from a fifth-floor window in a suicide attempt. Her parents have spent several years trying to intervene and stop their daughter from making the irreversible decision.

‌



According to her family, Ramos had been diagnosed with Borderline Personality Disorder. She spent various periods of her life in institutional care and was "relying on ... the Spanish mental healthcare system" prior to the rape.

View 2 Images Ramos' father has been trying to stop the euthanasia from happening (Image: X)

Euthanasia gang rape victim's defiant message to dad before ending life READ MORE :

In 2022, while living in a state-supervised centre for vulnerable young people, Ramos was brutally gang raped. The harrowing ordeal left her profoundly traumatised, further compounding the mental health struggles she had already been battling. Struggling to cope with the aftermath of the assault, Ramos attempted to end her own life by jumping from a building. She survived but was left paralysed from the waist down. Since then, she has suffered constant physical pain and severe disability, according to reports. Additionally, her mental health has worsened, with depression, obsessive-compulsive disorder, and borderline personality disorder, reports the Express.

‌



In February 2026, Spain's Constitutional Court rejected her father's appeal, stating there was "no violation of fundamental rights" and that the euthanasia could proceed.

Spain's Constitutional Court ruled to grant her euthanasia request, dismissing her father's challenge and confirming there was "no violation of fundamental rights" and that the procedure could go ahead.

The appeal was rejected under the Organic Law on the Regulation of Euthanasia of 2021. This legislation allows euthanasia in cases of serious and prolonged suffering. Since it came into force, hundreds have requested assistance in ending their lives.

‌



Ramos' mother, Yolanda Ramos, told the media, "I do not agree, but I will always be by her side."

In an interview just four days before her death, the 25 year old confirmed she never had any doubts about the procedure, saying, "I was very clear about it from the beginning.

"None of my family is in favour of euthanasia. Obviously, because I'm another pillar of the family. I'm leaving, and you're staying here with all the pain. But I think, all the pain I've suffered over the years... I just want to leave in peace now and stop suffering, full stop.

Article continues below

"And a father's, or a mother's, or a sister's happiness doesn't have to come before a daughter's happiness or sadness of a daughter's life."

News.title: Inside world's first hotel staffed by futuristic robot dinosaur receptionists
News.content:
Inside world's first hotel staffed by futuristic robot dinosaur receptionists

One hotel is offering a unique experience when it comes to its check-in staff. Instead of humans, guests at the hotel are greeted by robot dinosaurs to help them check-in to their room

View 4 Images Guests at one hotel based in Japan are greeted by robot dinosaurs to help them check-in (Image: Henn na Resort & Spa)

Most of us like to encounter unique experiences when we travel, and one hotel based in Japan is certainly offering that with its check-in staff. Instead of humans, guests at the hotel are greeted by robot dinosaurs to help them check-in to their room. The unique experience was shared over on Instagram, with influencer Anna-Lynne posting a reel about it. Her video saw her walk in to a ocean-themed room as robot dinosaurs greeted her at the check-in desk.

‌



Overlay text read, 'POV, it's 8pm in Japan, you're tired and you just want to check in to your hotel...' Following up in the caption, she wrote: "Booked this stay not knowing I’d be checked in by dinosaurs and low-key, I was terrified.

‌



It was 8pm, I was exhausted after a flight, and I had to walk from the train station in pitch dark just to get here. Then these prehistoric receptionists welcome me and I was like uhhh the f**k.

‌



View 4 Images The unique, high-tech hotel is in the Izumisano area (Image: Henn na Resort & Spa)

View 4 Images In addition to standard rooms, the hotel offers unique concept rooms (Image: Henn na Resort & Spa)

"But real talk they were somehow more polite than some humans I know. The check-in was smooth, and this place is super clean and quirky in the best way."

‌



The hotel in question is Henn na Resort & Spa Kansai Airport in Japan.

The unique, high-tech hotel in the Izumisano area is famous for being the world's first hotel brand staffed primarily by robots, including dinosaur receptionists.

It is located along the coast with scenic views of Osaka Bay and airplanes landing at the nearby airport.

‌



As well as guests being greeted and checked in by multilingual dinosaur robots in the ocean-themed lobby, they can also make use of many facilities such as indoor and open-air baths with bay views, a mineral mist sauna, and a dry sauna.

The on-site restaurant, Seian (Aoi-an), serves Japanese cuisine made with local ingredients and is supervised by a Michelin 1-star chef.

View 4 Images The hotel is often highly praised for its magnificent views (Image: Henn na Resort & Spa)

‌



In addition to standard rooms, the hotel offers unique concept rooms like Ninja, Pirate, and Peach Aviation collaboration.

The hotel is approximately 15 minutes by car or one train stop (6 minutes) from Kansai International Airport (KIX). It is a 10-minute walk from Rinku Town Station.

Attractions nearby include Rinku Premium Outlets, Rinku Park, and the Seacle shopping mall, which are all within walking distance.

‌



The hotel is often highly praised for its magnificent views, clean modern rooms, and the fun novelty of the robot staff. However, some guests found the walk from the station difficult with heavy luggage and noted that the automated check-in may occasionally require human assistance.

Content cannot be displayed without consent

People were loving the look of the robot dinosaur staff from Anna-Lynne's video, with it racking up more than 811,000 likes.

‌



One person exclaimed: "Wait this is so epic."

While another added: "My introverted self would prefer the awkward robot dinosaurs."

However, many others predicted they'd encounter a few issues too.

Article continues below

"Lol, this would send me over the edge of laughter and despair," one person wrote.

While another added: "Imagine trying to complain about room service and staying serious."

News.title: 'I found something in shower then had 35pc chance of death'
News.content:
'I found something in shower then had 35pc chance of death'

Vicky Keep, 51, was wondering how she would tell her children

View 4 Images Vicky having chemotherapy for the first time (Image: PA Real Life)

A mum-of-two was left wondering how she'd tell her children when she realised she had a '35 per cent chance of dying'. But now she is commemorating the 10th anniversary of her cancer diagnosis by embarking on a canoeing expedition along Sweden's Harken river, wild camping throughout the journey, as cancer has taught her to "not take life for granted anymore".

‌



Vicky Keep, 51, was diagnosed with stage three lobular breast cancer in 2016, after discovering "quite a big lump" in her breast while in the shower. After undergoing a single mastectomy, six months of chemotherapy and daily radiotherapy, she was informed she had overcome cancer in January 2018.

‌



Alongside two close friends, Vicky will paddle the entire length of the Harken river in northern Sweden this September, tackling rapids, portaging through dams, and surviving in the wilderness for eight days, in a challenge she describes as "not going to be a breeze, but something that will feel like a big achievement when we do complete it".

‌



She aims to raise £10,000 for Odyssey, an "incredible charity" that supports people with cancer to rediscover their zest for life. "Reaching 10 years is something that I've got to celebrate," Vicky said.

Vicky, who lives in Whitstable, Kent, and works in catering, found a lump in her left breast in May 2016 when, after a friend mentioned she had discovered a lump in her own breast, she realised she'd never examined herself before. That evening, while showering, she felt "quite a big lump", but presumed it "can't be anything sinister", as she believed breast cancer lumps were typically pea-sized.

View 4 Images Vicky underwent a gruelling 19 months of cancer treatment and surgeries (Image: PA Real Life)

‌



She waited a month, thinking it "might just go", but eventually visited her GP. She was quickly referred for a mammogram, recalling "that was the first point when I thought it might be something sinister, because there were two nurses in there who were looking at each other with a mild look of concern".

After an ultrasound and biopsies, she attended a consultant appointment at 4pm on June 22, 2016, assuming that "they're not going to tell me any bad news that late in the day". Instead, she was diagnosed with lobular breast cancer that had spread to her lymph nodes.

"You just don't see it coming," said Vicky, a mum of two daughters aged 17 and 21 who is celebrating her 25th wedding anniversary this year with her husband, Gary.

‌



"I remember just sitting there hearing these words: 'Sorry, it is breast cancer'. They'd also found a tumour in my armpit as well, and it was quite a large tumour.

"My first question to him was: 'How am I going to tell my girls?'. They were seven and just turned 12 at the time... you immediately just assume you're going to die. And I did have one of the most aggressive types of cancer. It was stage three, grade three. So when you actually looked at the statistics, the five-year survival rates were actually quite low."

Vicky underwent a mastectomy three weeks later, during which her affected lymph nodes were also removed, and six weeks following the operation she started chemotherapy. Following six months of chemotherapy, she took a month's break before commencing daily radiotherapy for a month.

‌



View 4 Images Vicky wanted to ‘do something big’ to mark a decade from her diagnosis (Image: PA Real Life)

She was informed that with the surgery, chemotherapy and radiotherapy, her five-year survival rate was approximately 65 per cent, but "the way I was looking at that was like, well, that's a 35 per cent chance of me dying". However, in January 2018, Vicky received the news that there was No Evidence of Disease (Ned) following a punishing 19 months of cancer treatment and operations.

"Now life is completely different. I very rarely think about cancer, but I didn't think I'd be here to be saying that," she said.

‌



Throughout the past eight years, Vicky has undertaken numerous challenges to raise funds for cancer charities, such as Cancer Research's Walk All Over Cancer, where she and her family walked 10,000 steps daily throughout March 2018. To commemorate the 10th anniversary of her diagnosis, Vicky wanted to "do something big", something that would generate a game-changing sum of money for a lesser-known cancer charity.

Vicky first encountered Odyssey shortly after her cancer treatment, when she was invited to one of its retreats for individuals living with or who have survived cancer. The charity organises residential weeks away for people battling cancer, those who have completed treatment and are rebuilding their lives, or those facing a terminal diagnosis, where they "challenge themselves to do things which are out of their comfort zone".

"Had I known what was on the agenda for that week, I would never have signed up for it..." Vicky confessed, choosing not to reveal the itinerary to preserve the surprise for future attendees. "But it was such an incredible week that when I came back, it just kind of felt like my life was going to be okay. I might not survive five, 10 years, but I've got to make sure that the life that I'm living is worthwhile and counted. It gave me a completely different outlook on life."

‌



When Vicky discovered that Odyssey was organising a canoe expedition down Sweden's Harken river in September 2026, she decided it was precisely the challenge she needed and even enlisted two close friends to join her.

"I phoned my best friend... She was like, '100% – 10 years is something to be celebrated. Let's do something that will challenge us both'," Vicky shared.

Another close mate, who Vicky got to know through her local soul choir and discovered was battling cancer at the same time, undergoing the same surgery and treatments, also agreed to participate in the challenge. Besides canoeing the river's 150-kilometre stretch, the team will encounter various obstacles along the way.

‌



View 4 Images Vicky will canoe the Harken river with two of her close friends (Image: PA Real Life)

"There will be some small rapids in areas, and some dams that we have to use porterage to move the canoes between," Vicky detailed, adding that she was informed by someone who has previously canoed the river that "at one point you have to carry all your equipment three-and-a-half kilometres to the other side, where you have to then put your boats back in."

They'll be wild camping and cooking on open fires during their journey, and while temperatures hover around 12 degrees during the day in September, they can plummet close to zero at night. "I've never done wild camping," Vicky confessed.

‌



"It's not just wild camping. There are no toilets, so it's wild pooing and all that! That is panicking me beyond what I can imagine, I'm like, Oh, God, this is way out of my comfort zone!"

Vicky and her pals will kick off their training in April, and have been advised to do plenty of front crawl swimming to build up the muscles they need for rowing. They'll also do some sea swimming, "because that's more challenging and it's cold", as well as taking the kayaks out for extended sessions.

The team's goal is to raise a minimum of £1,500 each, which would cover their participation costs, but they aspire to raise a total of £10,000 so they can contribute some much-needed funds to Odyssey.

Article continues below

Vicky's fundraising page is available on GoFundMe.
